title: What Is a Design Brief and Why Your Architect Needs One
author: CITRA Associates
secondaryKeywords: how to write a design brief
metaDescription: A design brief is the single most useful document you can bring to your architect. Learn what to include, how to write it, and why it leads to better design outcomes.
excerpt: A design brief is the foundational document of any architectural project. This article explains what a brief should contain — people, site, programme, values, budget, and reference images — how to write one effectively, and why clients who prepare thoughtful briefs consistently get better buildings.

faqs.question: How long should a design brief be?
faqs.answer: A good design brief for a residential project is typically 2 to 5 pages — long enough to cover all the important information but short enough to be read carefully and referred to throughout the project. Including reference images as an appendix can add pages while keeping the written content focused and readable.

faqs.question: What if I do not know what I want yet — can I still prepare a design brief?
faqs.answer: Yes. The brief does not require you to have all the answers before the design process begins — that would defeat its purpose. Write about what you do know: your site, your family's composition, your lifestyle, and your non-negotiable requirements. Your architect will work with this foundation and help you discover the answers to the questions you have not yet been able to answer yourself.

faqs.question: Can the design brief change during the project?
faqs.answer: Yes, within reason. Briefs evolve as clients learn through the design process, which is normal and expected. However, substantial brief changes after schematic design is complete will require redesign work and typically incur additional fees. The aim is to get the brief as complete and accurate as possible before significant design investment is made.
